steps.title: Haematology and oncology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Tertiary Care

steps.title: Non-Trauma Bleeding
steps.text: A 6-year-old boy is diagnosed with a factor VIII deficiency (Haemophilia A) after presenting with spontaneous joint bleeding. What is the next most appropriate step in management at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Once a specific clotting factor deficiency is identified, appropriate factor replacement is the cornerstone of treatment. Counselling the family, including education and discussing genetic implications, is essential at this level.

textChoices.text: Start oral iron supplements and observe
Administer factor VIII concentrate and counsel the family

steps.title: Non-Trauma Bleeding
steps.text: In a child diagnosed with a bleeding disorder, what additional evaluation is recommended at the tertiary level to support long-term management?
steps.rationale: Genetic bleeding disorders often warrant family screening. Multidisciplinary management involving haematology, social work, and potentially genetics ensures comprehensive care and support.

textChoices.text: Monitor bleeding only during hospital stays
Test siblings for similar deficiencies and plan multidisciplinary care
Repeat blood transfusions monthly regardless of symptoms

steps.title: Haemophilia
steps.text: Which of the following best explains the occurrence of severe haemophilia in females?
steps.rationale: Severe haemophilia in females is rare and typically caused by genetic factors such as X chromosome abnormalities or inheritance from both carrier parents.

textChoices.text: Inheritance of pathogenic variants from both parents or X-chromosome abnormalities

steps.title: Haemophilia
steps.text: Which of the following is the preferred treatment for haemophilia A in a tertiary care setting?
steps.rationale: Factor VIII concentrate is the standard and most effective treatment for haemophilia A in advanced healthcare settings.

steps.title: Haemophilia
steps.text: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding desmopressin (DDAVP)?
steps.rationale: DDAVP raises FVIII and vWF levels but has no effect on FIX; thus, it is ineffective in haemophilia B.

textChoices.text: It is ineffective in haemophilia B because it does not increase Factor IX
It is used primarily in haemophilia B
It is contraindicated in haemophilia A

steps.title: Haemophilia
steps.text: What is the recommended dosing frequency of tranexamic acid in managing bleeding in haemophilia?
steps.rationale: Tranexamic acid is an antifibrinolytic used frequently in mucosal bleeding. Multiple daily doses ensure sustained therapeutic levels.

steps.title: Haemophilia
steps.text: Which of the following is TRUE about Emicizumab?
steps.rationale: Emicizumab mimics the function of FVIII and is used for prophylaxis in haemophilia A patients, including those with inhibitors.

textChoices.text: It is only used in haemophilia B
It is effective in haemophilia A including patients with FVIII inhibitors
It directly replaces Factor VIII in the blood

steps.title: Oncological Emergencies
steps.text: What is the recommended timing for initiating IV antibiotics in a child presenting with oncological emergency and suspected infection?
steps.rationale: In oncological emergencies, early broad-spectrum IV antibiotic treatment (ideally within 30 minutes of presentation) is crucial. Delays increase the risk of morbidity and mortality in neutropenic or immunocompromised children.

textChoices.text: Only after blood culture results are available
After ruling out fungal or viral causes

steps.title: Oncological Emergencies
steps.text: Which of the following is the correct initial antibiotic regimen for a pediatric oncology patient with suspected sepsis, according to guidelines?
steps.rationale: The recommended empirical treatment includes IV Benzylpenicillin and IV Gentamicin, with antifungal (Fluconazole) and antiviral (Acyclovir) coverage depending on the case. Modifications are made based on culture results.

textChoices.text: IV Benzylpenicillin and Gentamicin, with Fluconazole ± Acyclovir

steps.title: Oncological Emergencies
steps.text: What is the primary purpose of doing a Full Blood Count in a child with oncological emergency?
steps.rationale: FBC is essential in oncological emergencies to assess neutropenia, which guides urgency and intensity of treatment, especially antibiotic administration.

textChoices.text: To determine blood group before transfusion
To assess the degree of neutropenia

steps.title: Tumour Lysis Syndrome
steps.text: Which of the following medications acts by converting uric acid into a more soluble compound and is preferred for rapid reduction in severe tumour lysis syndrome?
steps.rationale: Rasburicase directly degrades uric acid into allantoin and is more effective for acute TLS than allopurinol, which only blocks new uric acid formation.

steps.title: Tumour Lysis Syndrome
steps.text: What is the target urine output in TLS management using hyperhydration protocols?
steps.rationale: High urine output is critical to flush metabolites; hence, fluid therapy targets 100 mL/m²/hour urine output.

steps.title: Tumour Lysis Syndrome
steps.text: Which of the following is NOT appropriate in the initial management of hyperkalaemia in TLS?
steps.rationale: Administering potassium is contraindicated in TLS where hyperkalaemia is a core issue.

steps.title: Tumour Lysis Syndrome
steps.text: In TLS, when should calcium be given for hypocalcaemia?
steps.rationale: Treating hypocalcaemia while phosphate is still high risks calcium-phosphate precipitation and organ damage.

textChoices.text: Only if symptomatic and hyperphosphatemia is corrected
Only if phosphorus levels are low
Only in combination with insulin and glucose

steps.title: Tumour Lysis Syndrome
steps.text: Which of the following is NOT an indication for dialysis in TLS?
steps.rationale: Asymptomatic hypocalcaemia does not require dialysis. Symptomatic and complex biochemical derangements do.

steps.title: Spinal Cord Compression
steps.text: Which of the following is the appropriate initial dose of dexamethasone for a child with spinal cord compression at the tertiary care level?
steps.rationale: High-dose dexamethasone is initiated immediately to reduce inflammation and spinal cord edema. Continued dosing every hour helps maintain the anti-inflammatory effect.

steps.title: Spinal Cord Compression
steps.text: What complication should be closely monitored in a child with spinal cord compression secondary to malignancy such as Burkitt’s lymphoma?
steps.rationale: Rapid cell lysis in hematological malignancies like Burkitt’s lymphoma can lead to TLS, a life-threatening metabolic complication.

steps.title: Spinal Cord Compression
steps.text: Which of the following is an appropriate next step in managing spinal cord compression with confirmed epidural mass at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Epidural masses causing compression require urgent decompression—either surgically or via radiotherapy—to prevent permanent neurological deficits.

steps.title: Superior Vena Cava Compressions Syndrome
steps.text: Which of the following medications should be started empirically in a child with suspected SVC compression while awaiting oncologic diagnosis?
steps.rationale: Empirical steroids (prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day in two doses) help reduce tumour mass compressing the SVC, and allopurinol (10 mg/kg/day in 3 doses) prevents tumour lysis complications before chemotherapy.

steps.title: Superior Vena Cava Compressions Syndrome
steps.text: In managing a child with Superior Vena Cava Syndrome due to a mediastinal mass, why is early consultation with an oncologist essential, even if empirical treatment has already started?
steps.rationale: While empirical steroids and supportive care are initiated early, targeted chemotherapy requires oncologic input based on tumour type (e.g. lymphoma, leukemia). Early consultation ensures appropriate and timely treatment.

textChoices.text: Because steroids alone are usually curative in SVC syndrome
To ensure timely administration of radiotherapy before chemotherapy
To guide specific chemotherapy based on underlying tumour type
Because surgical decompression is the only effective treatment

steps.title: Hyperleukocytosis
steps.text: Which of the following interventions should be AVOIDED in the initial management of hyperleukocytosis due to risk of increased blood viscosity?
steps.rationale: Packed RBC transfusions increase blood viscosity, worsening leukostasis and organ perfusion risks. Avoid unless absolutely necessary.

textChoices.text: Administering platelet transfusions when count is <20,000/µL
Transfusion of packed red blood cells

steps.title: Hyperleukocytosis
steps.text: What is the most appropriate fluid management strategy for a child with hyperleukocytosis?
steps.rationale: Hyperhydration helps prevent tumor lysis-related renal failure. Potassium-containing fluids must be avoided due to TLS risk.

textChoices.text: Maintenance IV fluids with added potassium
Oral rehydration therapy with glucose solution
IV normal saline with 5% dextrose at twice the maintenance rate
IV fluids restricted to avoid fluid overload

steps.title: Hyperleukocytosis
steps.text: A child with hyperleukocytosis has a urine output of 0.4 mL/kg/hour. What is the best next step?
steps.rationale: Adequate urine output is critical to manage TLS and prevent renal complications. Maintaining 1–2 mL/kg/hour is the target.

textChoices.text: Reduce fluid rate to prevent overload
Adjust fluids to achieve 1–2 mL/kg/hour urine output

steps.title: Wilms Tumour / Nephroblastoma
steps.text: Which of the following best represents the definitive treatment approach for Wilms Tumour at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The definitive treatment for Wilms Tumour at the tertiary level includes chemotherapy and surgical resection of the tumor. Radiotherapy may be added depending on the stage and histological subtype.

textChoices.text: Chemotherapy, surgery, with or without radiotherapy

steps.title: Retinoblastoma
steps.text: What is the primary goal of treatment in children diagnosed with retinoblastoma?
steps.rationale: The foremost aim in retinoblastoma treatment is to save the child’s life, especially in advanced or extraocular disease. Vision preservation is a secondary goal where feasible.

textChoices.text: Preservation of vision in both eyes

steps.title: Retinoblastoma
steps.text: Which of the following chemotherapy regimens is typically used for extraocular retinoblastoma?
steps.rationale: For extraocular retinoblastoma, six cycles of chemotherapy (pre- and post-enucleation) are typically used, involving drugs such as vincristine, etoposide, and carboplatin.

textChoices.text: Two cycles of vincristine and etoposide
Six cycles—three preoperative and three postoperative

steps.title: Retinoblastoma
steps.text: Which surgical procedure is typically performed when there is no chance of preserving vision in the affected eye?
steps.rationale: Enucleation involves removing the affected eye and is the mainstay surgery for advanced intraocular retinoblastoma when vision cannot be saved.

steps.title: Retinoblastoma
steps.text: In which of the following cases is external beam radiation therapy (EBRT) most appropriate for retinoblastoma?
steps.rationale: EBRT is reserved for complex cases such as vitreous seeding, progression despite chemo-reduction, or tumour extension beyond the optic nerve margin.

steps.title: Retinoblastoma
steps.text: Which treatment is best suited for a small anteriorly located tumour in retinoblastoma?
steps.rationale: Cryotherapy is effective for treating small tumours located anteriorly by inducing tumour necrosis via freezing.

steps.title: Lymphoma
steps.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial approach for performing procedures in a child with a mediastinal mass?
steps.rationale: Children with mediastinal masses are at high risk of airway or cardiovascular collapse in the supine position. Procedures like imaging or biopsies should be performed in side-lying or prone positions to mitigate this risk.

textChoices.text: Perform all procedures under general anesthesia
Proceed with CT scans and BMAT in supine position
Perform procedures in lateral or prone position to reduce risk of airway collapse
Delay all procedures until complete chemotherapy is initiated

steps.title: Lymphoma
steps.text: A child with lymphoma is at risk of tumour lysis syndrome. Which of the following is an appropriate component of prevention and management?
steps.rationale: Preventing tumour lysis syndrome involves aggressive IV hydration with potassium-free fluids, as potassium release from lysed cells can cause dangerous hyperkalemia.

textChoices.text: Delay hydration until diagnosis is confirmed
Provide vigorous IV hydration with potassium-free fluids
Withhold allopurinol until uric acid levels are elevated

steps.title: Lymphoma
steps.text: Which of the following is a key early management step in a child with suspected spinal cord compression due to lymphoma?
steps.rationale: Early steroid administration (e.g., dexamethasone) helps reduce inflammation and spinal cord compression. Neurological status must be closely monitored.

textChoices.text: Dexamethasone administration followed by close neurological monitoring
Delay treatment until MRI confirms the diagnosis
Lumbar puncture to relieve spinal pressure

steps.title: Lymphoma
steps.text: Which of the following is TRUE regarding initiation of chemotherapy in children with lymphoma at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: A low-dose pre-phase helps reduce tumour burden safely before initiating aggressive multi-agent chemotherapy. CNS-directed therapy is part of standard protocols, not limited to relapse.

textChoices.text: High-dose chemotherapy should be given immediately without pre-phase
Chemotherapy should be delayed until biopsy results return
Treatment begins with a low-dose pre-phase followed by multi-agent high-dose therapy
CNS-directed therapy is only indicated in relapsed disease

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: Which of the following is an indication for escalating the dose of hydroxyurea in a child with SCD already on treatment?
steps.rationale: Stroke is a clear indication for hydroxyurea dose escalation up to the tolerated maximum, per guidelines.

textChoices.text: One mild pain crisis in past year

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: When escalating hydroxyurea, what is the maximum allowed increase in dose at each step?
steps.rationale: Escalation must be slow and safe—no more than 5 mg/kg/day every 8 weeks.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: Before escalating hydroxyurea, what lab value confirms sufficient marrow response?
steps.rationale: MCV ≥100 reflects effective marrow response and is used as a surrogate for adherence.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A child with SCD presents with seizures and right-sided weakness. What is the preferred transfusion approach if Hb is 9 g/dL?
steps.rationale: If Hb >8 g/dL and stroke present, exchange transfusion reduces HbS without causing hyperviscosity.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: When managing stroke in a child with Hb = 6.5 g/dL, the best immediate transfusion approach is:
steps.rationale: For Hb ≤8 g/dL and stroke, a simple transfusion is indicated—goal Hb = 10 g/dL.

textChoices.text: Defer transfusion until CT is done

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A painful crisis without fever in SCD should be managed with:
steps.rationale: Uncomplicated pain crisis doesn’t require antibiotics or transfusion unless other symptoms arise.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: In a patient with suspected osteomyelitis, x-ray imaging is best done:
steps.rationale: X-ray changes of osteomyelitis may not appear for 10–14 days. Delay imaging until after analgesia trial fails.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A 3-year-old with SCD presents with pallor, splenomegaly, and Hb drop from 9 → 5 g/dL. What is the immediate action?
steps.rationale: This is splenic sequestration, a life-threatening emergency requiring urgent transfusion and monitoring.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: During transfusion for splenic sequestration, why must volume be carefully controlled?
steps.rationale: Sudden release of pooled blood after transfusion may raise Hb rapidly and increase viscosity risk.

textChoices.text: To prevent hyperviscosity from pooled blood release

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: How frequently should vitals be monitored during acute splenic sequestration?
steps.rationale: Frequent monitoring is essential due to risk of rapid decompensation and shock.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A 13-year-old with SCD presents with 2-hour priapism. First-line management includes:
steps.rationale: Early management focuses on hydration, warm compresses, pain relief—before escalation.

textChoices.text: IV fluids + oral hydration + warm compress

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: What is the preferred pharmacologic intervention if priapism does not resolve after 2 hours?
steps.rationale: If early measures fail, oral alpha-agonist like pseudoephedrine can relieve stuttering priapism.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: A child with persistent priapism >4–6 hours and turbid glans penis may require:
steps.rationale: In this case, corpus spongiosum is affected and a dorsal vein–corpora cavernosa shunt is needed.

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: In a boy with priapism, which clinical sign suggests no shunt is needed?
steps.rationale: If glans is soft and no retention, spongiosum is unaffected; a shunt isn’t needed.

textChoices.text: Soft glans and no urinary retention

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: In children with severe acute malnutrition requiring transfusion, how should blood be administered?
steps.rationale: In SAM, fluid overload is dangerous. Limit to 10 mL/kg and avoid transfusing more than 20 mL/kg in 4 days.

textChoices.text: 10 mL/kg of packed cells slowly, avoid >20 mL/kg in 4 days

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: What clinical feature would prompt transfusion in a child with Hb = 5.2 g/dL and Hct = 15%?
steps.rationale: Children with moderate anaemia (Hb 4–6 g/dL) require transfusion if clinical signs of severity are present.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: A 9-year-old girl presents with anaemia and heavy menstruation. What is the next best step?
steps.rationale: Menorrhagia is a common cause of iron-deficiency anaemia and needs further evaluation.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: A child was started on ferrous sulphate but shows poor tolerance. What’s the next best step?
steps.rationale: If one oral iron form is not tolerated, switch to another (e.g., ferrous gluconate).

textChoices.text: Try a different oral iron preparation

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: A neonate presents with pallor, hypotension, and Hb = 2.8 g/dL. What is the immediate management?
steps.rationale: Severely affected neonates with haemorrhage and very low Hb require transfusion urgently.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: What lab finding suggests intrauterine haemorrhage in neonatal anaemia?
steps.rationale: Nucleated RBCs reflect fetal response to blood loss and are commonly raised in neonatal haemorrhage.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: Which test is appropriate when congenital syphilis is suspected as a cause of neonatal anaemia?
steps.rationale: Congenital infection should be ruled out by testing both mother and neonate for syphilis.

textChoices.text: Syphilis test for both mother and child

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: Which finding supports a diagnosis of severe aplastic anaemia?
steps.rationale: Severe aplastic anaemia is defined by marrow cellularity <25% plus ≥2 cytopenias (including platelets <20k and neutrophils <500).

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: A child with pancytopenia and no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy most likely has:
steps.rationale: Aplastic anaemia often lacks lymphadenopathy or organomegaly—differentiating it from leukaemia.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: A child has a family history of early bone marrow failure and congenital anomalies. Which condition is likely?
steps.rationale: IBMFS are associated with family history, physical anomalies, and progressive marrow failure.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: Which of the following is an indication to refer anaemia to a haematologist at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Neonatal anaemias, especially with uncertain or pathological causes, require specialist review.

textChoices.text: Anaemia responding to oral iron after 2 weeks
Neonatal anaemia due to twin-to-twin transfusion
